---
title: "Modern MDO Landscape"
---

```{julia}
#| include: false
include("src/bliss_rs.jl")
include("src/bliss_gp.jl")
using Printf, Plots, LaTeXStrings, Statistics, LinearAlgebra, Random, Distributions
gr()
```

## MDO Decomposition: A Taxonomy

BLISS is one of several decomposition architectures that have been developed over
the past four decades to manage the complexity of multidisciplinary system design.
@fig-mdo-taxonomy maps the family.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-mdo-taxonomy
%%| fig-cap: "MDO architecture taxonomy. Architectures differ on where system-level consistency constraints are enforced (MDF: within the inner loop; IDF/SAND: explicitly as outer constraints) and how information flows between disciplines (sequential vs. concurrent)."
%%| fig-width: 7

flowchart LR
    MDO["MDO\nArchitectures"]

    subgraph mono ["  Monolithic  "]
        MDF["MDF\nMultidisciplinary\nFeasible"]
        IDF["IDF\nIndividual\nDiscipline Feasible"]
        SAND["SAND\nSimultaneous\nAnalysis & Design"]
    end

    subgraph decomp ["  Decomposition-Based  "]
        CO["CO\nCollaborative\nOptimisation"]
        CSSO["CSSO\nConcurrent\nSubspace Opt."]
        BLISSARCH["BLISS\nBi-Level Integrated\nSystem Synthesis"]
        ATC["ATC\nAnalytical\nTarget Cascading"]
    end

    MDO --> mono & decomp

    classDef arch  fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#1e3a5f
    classDef bliss fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532d
    classDef title fill:#f1f5f9,stroke:#94a3b8,stroke-width:1px,color:#0f172a

    class MDF,IDF,SAND,CO,CSSO,ATC arch
    class BLISSARCH bliss
    class MDO title
```

### MDF, IDF, and SAND

**MDF (Multidisciplinary Feasible)** enforces disciplinary consistency at every
system analysis call. Couplings are satisfied implicitly by a fixed-point (Gauss-Seidel)
or Newton iteration. The system optimizer sees only converged analyses but pays the
cost of full convergence each function evaluation.

**IDF (Individual Discipline Feasible)** introduces explicit coupling variables as
optimization unknowns. Disciplines run independently without iteration, and coupling
consistency is enforced by the outer optimizer as equality constraints. This enables
parallelism at the expense of a larger (but well-structured) optimization problem.

**SAND (Simultaneous Analysis and Design)** treats all state and design variables as
optimization variables simultaneously. The optimizer solves the governing equations
and the design problem jointly. Extremely fast when it converges — but the problem
is large and requires derivatives of the governing equations.

### Where BLISS Sits

BLISS is a two-level decomposition that inherits IDF's independent disciplinary
structure but adds the distinction between system-level ($Z$) and local ($X$)
variables. The key innovation is that the system optimizer never calls the
disciplines directly — it operates entirely on surrogate models. This makes BLISS
uniquely suited to black-box disciplines: codes that cannot be differentiated or
whose source is unavailable.

## Multi-Fidelity Methods

The most impactful practical extension to BLISS-2026 is **multi-fidelity** modeling:
using a hierarchy of analysis tools of differing fidelity and cost to accelerate
convergence.

### Kennedy-O'Hagan Co-Kriging

Kennedy and O'Hagan (2000) [@sobieski1998bliss] proposed a principled Bayesian
framework for combining models at multiple fidelity levels. For two levels (cheap
$f_L$ and expensive $f_H$):

$$
f_H(\mathbf{x}) = \rho \cdot f_L(\mathbf{x}) + \delta(\mathbf{x})
$$ {#eq-cokriging}

where $\rho$ is a regression scaling coefficient and $\delta(\mathbf{x})$ is an
independent GP capturing the discrepancy between the two fidelity levels. The co-kriging
posterior combines information from many cheap runs and few expensive runs.

In a BLISS-2026 context:

| Level | Cost | Use |
|-------|------|-----|
| $f_L$ | Analytical surrogate (ms/call) | LHS initial DOE, EI candidates |
| $f_M$ | Medium CFD (minutes/call) | Confirm promising regions |
| $f_H$ | Full RANS + FEM (hours/call) | Final design validation only |

```{julia}
#| label: fig-multifidelity
#| fig-cap: "Multi-fidelity co-kriging illustration. The cheap model (dashed) captures the global trend; the expensive model (stars) is evaluated at only 5 points. Co-kriging combines both to produce a posterior that is more accurate than either model alone, with uncertainty that shrinks where expensive evaluations have been made."
#| code-fold: true

rng3 = MersenneTwister(55)
x_all = range(0, 1, length=300)

# True high-fidelity function
f_H(x) = sin(4π*x) * exp(-x) + 0.5*x^2

# Cheap low-fidelity (captures trend, misses detail)
f_L(x) = 0.85 * sin(4π*x) * exp(-x) + 0.3*x^2 + 0.1*sin(8π*x)

# Low-fidelity training (many cheap evals)
x_L   = collect(range(0, 1, length=25))
y_L   = f_L.(x_L)

# High-fidelity training (few expensive evals)
x_H   = [0.1, 0.3, 0.55, 0.75, 0.92]
y_H   = f_H.(x_H)

# Step 1: GP on low-fidelity
X_Lm  = reshape(x_L, :, 1)
gp_L, ym_L, ys_L = fit_gp_auto(X_Lm, y_L)
X_test3 = reshape(collect(x_all), :, 1)
μ_L, σ²_L = predict_gp_scaled(gp_L, ym_L, ys_L, X_test3)

# Step 2: GP on discrepancy δ(x) = f_H(x) - ρ*f_L(x)
ρ = 0.9
δ_H = y_H .- ρ .* f_L.(x_H)
X_Hm  = reshape(x_H, :, 1)
gp_δ, ym_δ, ys_δ = fit_gp_auto(X_Hm, δ_H)
μ_δ, σ²_δ = predict_gp_scaled(gp_δ, ym_δ, ys_δ, X_test3)

# Co-kriging prediction
μ_CK  = ρ .* μ_L .+ μ_δ
σ_CK  = sqrt.(max.(ρ^2 .* σ²_L .+ σ²_δ, 0.0))

p = plot(x_all, f_H.(x_all), lw=2, color=:black, label="True f_H(x)")
plot!(x_all, f_L.(x_all), lw=1.5, color=:gray, linestyle=:dash, label="Low-fidelity f_L(x)")
plot!(x_all, μ_CK, lw=2, color=:steelblue, label="Co-kriging mean")
plot!(x_all, μ_CK .- 2σ_CK, fillrange=μ_CK .+ 2σ_CK,
      fillalpha=0.18, color=:steelblue, lw=0, label="Co-kriging ±2σ")
scatter!(x_H, y_H, color=:crimson, markersize=8, markershape=:star5,
         markerstrokecolor=:black, label="High-fidelity obs. (5 pts)")
scatter!(x_L, y_L, color=:gray60, markersize=3, alpha=0.6, label="Low-fidelity obs.")
plot!(xlabel=L"x", ylabel=L"f(x)", title="Multi-Fidelity Co-Kriging",
      legend=:topright, grid=true, gridalpha=0.3)
```

The co-kriging mean in @fig-multifidelity is significantly more accurate than either
single-fidelity GP alone — it exploits the global shape information from 25 cheap
runs while correcting for systematic errors using just 5 expensive evaluations.

## Neural Network Surrogates

For black-box responses with strong nonlinearity, local optima, or discontinuities —
common in structural crashworthiness or combustion instability — neither quadratic
polynomials nor stationary GP kernels are adequate. **Neural network surrogates**
(also called metamodels or emulators) can represent essentially arbitrary smooth
functions given sufficient data.

A feedforward network with $L$ layers:

$$
\hat{f}(\mathbf{x}) = W_L \sigma(\cdots W_2 \sigma(W_1 \mathbf{x} + b_1) + b_2 \cdots) + b_L
$$ {#eq-nn}

### Julia Implementation (from scratch, no Flux dependency)

```{julia}
#| label: lst-nn-demo
#| code-fold: false

# Minimal 2-layer feedforward network in pure Julia
# Architecture: n_in → 16 → 16 → 1

struct TwoLayerNet
    W1::Matrix{Float64}; b1::Vector{Float64}
    W2::Matrix{Float64}; b2::Vector{Float64}
    W3::Matrix{Float64}; b3::Vector{Float64}
end

relu(x) = max.(x, 0.0)

function forward(net::TwoLayerNet, X::Matrix{Float64})
    h1 = relu.(net.W1 * X' .+ net.b1)
    h2 = relu.(net.W2 * h1 .+ net.b2)
    ŷ  = net.W3 * h2 .+ net.b3
    return vec(ŷ)
end

function init_net(n_in::Int; h=16, seed=42)
    rng = MersenneTwister(seed)
    s1, s2 = sqrt(2/n_in), sqrt(2/h)
    TwoLayerNet(
        randn(rng, h, n_in) * s1, zeros(h),
        randn(rng, h, h)    * s2, zeros(h),
        randn(rng, 1, h)    * s2, zeros(1),
    )
end

# MSE loss + gradient-free training (coordinate perturbation)
function train_nn(net::TwoLayerNet, X_train, y_train;
                  n_epochs=500, lr=0.05, seed=42)
    rng = MersenneTwister(seed)
    # Flatten parameters
    function net_to_vec(n)
        vcat(vec(n.W1), n.b1, vec(n.W2), n.b2, vec(n.W3), n.b3)
    end
    function vec_to_net(v, n)
        h, ni = size(n.W1)
        i = 0
        W1 = reshape(v[i+1:i+h*ni], h, ni); i += h*ni
        b1 = v[i+1:i+h];  i += h
        W2 = reshape(v[i+1:i+h*h], h, h);  i += h*h
        b2 = v[i+1:i+h];  i += h
        W3 = reshape(v[i+1:i+h], 1, h);    i += h
        b3 = v[i+1:i+1];
        TwoLayerNet(W1,b1,W2,b2,W3,b3)
    end
    mse(n) = mean((forward(n, X_train) .- y_train).^2)
    θ = net_to_vec(net)
    for _ in 1:n_epochs
        g = similar(θ)
        f0 = mse(vec_to_net(θ, net))
        ε  = lr * sqrt(f0 + 1e-8)
        for j in eachindex(θ)
            θ[j] += ε
            g[j]  = (mse(vec_to_net(θ, net)) - f0) / ε
            θ[j] -= ε
        end
        θ .-= lr * g
    end
    return vec_to_net(θ, net)
end

# Demo on Rosenbrock
rng4   = MersenneTwister(77)
n_demo = 100
X_demo = rand(rng4, n_demo, 2) .* 4 .- 2   # [-2,2]²
y_demo2= [100*(X_demo[i,2]-X_demo[i,1]^2)^2 + (X_demo[i,1]-1)^2
          for i in 1:n_demo]

net0    = init_net(2)
net_fit = train_nn(net0, X_demo, y_demo2; n_epochs=300, lr=0.005)

# Test accuracy
X_test4 = rand(rng4, 50, 2) .* 4 .- 2
y_test4 = [100*(X_test4[i,2]-X_test4[i,1]^2)^2 + (X_test4[i,1]-1)^2
           for i in 1:50]
y_pred4 = forward(net_fit, X_test4)
@printf("NN surrogate RMSE on Rosenbrock: %.1f\n",
        sqrt(mean((y_test4 .- y_pred4).^2)))
```

### When to Use Neural Networks vs. GPs

| Criterion | Use GP | Use NN |
|-----------|--------|--------|
| Training data budget | < 500 points | > 500 points |
| Dimension $n$ | ≤ 30 | Any |
| Uncertainty needed | Yes | Approximate (MC dropout) |
| Response smoothness | Smooth, unimodal | Rough, multimodal, discontinuous |
| Derivative needed | Analytic | Autodiff (ForwardDiff.jl) |
| Training cost | Seconds | Minutes–hours |

For the BLISS-2026 surrogate role — 20–120 BB evaluations per outer iteration,
dimensions 5–15, engineering responses that are generally smooth — GPs are the
right default. Neural networks become relevant for the SRM combustion instability
problem (Chapter 10) where the response has sharp pressure spikes near resonance.

## Uncertainty Quantification Propagation

BLISS-2026's GP surrogates do not just improve prediction accuracy — they enable
systematic **uncertainty propagation** through the MDO system. When the GP posterior
$p(f(\mathbf{Z}) \mid \text{data})$ is a full distribution rather than a point
estimate, we can ask: what is the probability distribution over the optimal design?

### Monte Carlo UQ Through the GP Posterior

```{julia}
#| label: fig-uq-propagation
#| fig-cap: "Uncertainty propagation through BLISS-2026. Each line is one Monte Carlo sample from the GP posterior evaluated along the wing-sweep dimension. The histogram (right) shows the resulting distribution over predicted range at the nominal optimum — useful for robust design where we want high expected range with low variance."
#| code-fold: true

# Rebuild the wing-sweep slice GP from scratch (chapter-local data)
include("src/supersonic_jet.jl")
Z_base2   = [0.075, 4.0, 45.0, 400.0, 120.0, 4.5, 0.2, 45_000.0, 1.8]
lo_sw_11, hi_sw_11 = 35.0, 72.0
sweep_range_11 = range(lo_sw_11, hi_sw_11, length=200)

rng_11 = MersenneTwister(42)
x_tr_11 = sort(lo_sw_11 .+ rand(rng_11, 10) .* (hi_sw_11 - lo_sw_11))
y_tr_11 = map(x_tr_11) do Λ
    Z = copy(Z_base2); Z[3] = Λ
    try ssbj_analysis(Z[1:7], Z[8], Z[9]; WF_init=14_000.0, WT_empty=28_000.0).Range
    catch; 0.0 end
end
x_c_11 = (x_tr_11 .- lo_sw_11) ./ (hi_sw_11 - lo_sw_11)

# GP trained on wing-sweep slice
X_mat3 = reshape(x_c_11, 10, 1)
gp_sweep, ym3, ys3 = fit_gp_auto(X_mat3, y_tr_11)
n_fine = 200
X_fine = reshape(collect(range(0, 1, n_fine)), n_fine, 1)
sweep_plot = collect(sweep_range_11)   # 200-element vector

# Draw GP posterior samples
μ_sw, σ²_sw = predict_gp_scaled(gp_sweep, ym3, ys3, X_fine)
σ_sw = sqrt.(max.(σ²_sw, 0.0))

rng5 = MersenneTwister(123)
n_mc = 80

p1 = plot(sweep_plot, μ_sw, lw=2, color=:steelblue, label="GP mean",
          xlabel="Wing sweep Λ_w (deg)", ylabel="Range (NM)",
          title="GP Posterior Samples", legend=:topleft)
for _ in 1:n_mc
    f_sample = μ_sw .+ σ_sw .* randn(rng5, n_fine)
    plot!(sweep_plot, f_sample, color=:steelblue, alpha=0.07, lw=0.7, label="")
end
scatter!(x_tr_11, y_tr_11, color=:black, markersize=5, label="Observations")

# Distribution at nominal optimal sweep ≈ 55°
x_opt_sw = (55.0 - lo_sw_11) / (hi_sw_11 - lo_sw_11)
μ_opt, σ²_opt = predict_gp_scaled(gp_sweep, ym3, ys3, reshape([x_opt_sw], 1, 1))
d_opt = Normal(μ_opt[1], sqrt(σ²_opt[1]))
r_range = range(μ_opt[1] - 4*sqrt(σ²_opt[1]), μ_opt[1] + 4*sqrt(σ²_opt[1]), length=200)

p2 = plot(pdf.(d_opt, r_range), r_range, fill=(0, 0.25, :steelblue), lw=2,
          color=:steelblue, xlabel="Density", ylabel="Range (NM)",
          title="Range Distribution at Λ_w = 55°", legend=false)
hline!([μ_opt[1]], color=:crimson, lw=2, linestyle=:dash)
annotate!(maximum(pdf.(d_opt, r_range))*0.5, μ_opt[1]-60,
          text(@sprintf("σ = %.0f NM", sqrt(σ²_opt[1])), 9, :crimson))

plot(p1, p2, layout=(1,2), size=(780, 340))
```

### Robust Optimisation

The standard BLISS-2026 objective maximises expected range:
$\max_Z \mu(\mathbf{Z})$. A *robust* variant instead maximises:

$$
\max_{\mathbf{Z}} \left[\mu(\mathbf{Z}) - \lambda\,\sigma(\mathbf{Z})\right]
$$ {#eq-robust-obj}

where $\lambda > 0$ is a risk-aversion parameter. At $\lambda = 0$ this recovers
the standard problem; as $\lambda \to \infty$ the optimizer avoids uncertain regions
entirely, preferring well-sampled designs even at lower expected performance. In
practice $\lambda = 1$ to $2$ gives a good balance.

## Digital Twins and BLISS

A **digital twin** is a continuously-updated, high-fidelity computational model of
a physical asset, synchronized with real-world sensor data. For an SRM test program,
a digital twin might consist of:

- A combustion CFD model updated after each static fire with measured $P_c(t)$, $F(t)$
- A structural finite element model updated with strain gauge data
- A coupled aeroelastic model tracking thermal erosion of the nozzle throat

BLISS-2026 maps naturally onto the digital-twin architecture:

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-digital-twin
%%| fig-cap: "BLISS-2026 in a digital-twin context. Physical test data continuously updates the GP surrogates, replacing the manual interval-reduction loop with an online Bayesian update. Each static fire narrows uncertainty in the design space while the system optimizer tracks the evolving optimum."
%%| fig-width: 6

flowchart LR
    HW["Physical\nSRM Test Stand"]
    Sensor["Data Acquisition\nP_c(t), F(t), strain"]
    GP_update["GP Posterior\nUpdate"]
    BLISS["BLISS-2026\nSystem Optimizer"]
    Design["Updated\nDesign Z*"]
    Next["Next Motor\nConfiguration"]

    HW --> Sensor --> GP_update --> BLISS --> Design --> Next --> HW

    classDef physical fill:#fff1f2,stroke:#e11d48,stroke-width:2px,color:#4c0519
    classDef data     fill:#fefce8,stroke:#ca8a04,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#422006
    classDef gp       fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#14532d
    classDef opt      fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#7c3aed,stroke-width:2px,color:#2e1065

    class HW,Next physical
    class Sensor,Design data
    class GP_update gp
    class BLISS opt
```

In this closed loop:
- After each physical test, the measured performance data updates the GP surrogate
  as new training observations (sequential Bayesian updating).
- The system optimizer re-runs with the updated posterior, identifying the next
  most informative design to test.
- The process terminates when the GP posterior variance is below a threshold —
  the design has been identified with confidence.

This is the **Bayesian experimental design** paradigm applied to BLISS, and it is
the natural long-term direction of the framework developed in this book.

## Reinforcement Learning for MDO

An emerging research direction treats MDO as a **sequential decision problem** and
applies reinforcement learning (RL). The optimizer is an RL agent that learns a
policy $\pi(a \mid s)$ mapping the current design state $s$ (observed BB outputs,
interval bounds, GP uncertainty map) to a design action $a$ (next $Z$ to evaluate).

Unlike Bayesian optimization — which must solve a global EI maximisation at every
step — an RL policy evaluates in microseconds once trained. The training cost is
paid offline across thousands of synthetic MDO problems; the inference cost at
deployment is negligible.

Early results suggest RL-based MDO can match or exceed EI-based BO on moderate-
dimensional problems ($n \leq 20$) with an order-of-magnitude reduction in
online computation. This is an active research frontier as of 2026.

## Summary: Where MDO Is Heading

| Trend | Impact on BLISS-2026 |
|-------|---------------------|
| **GP + EI** (this book) | Better surrogates, adaptive sampling, UQ |
| **Multi-fidelity co-kriging** | 10–100× cheaper per iteration |
| **Neural network surrogates** | Handle rough, high-dim responses |
| **Digital twins** | Online GP updates from sensor data |
| **Robust optimization** | Designs robust to model uncertainty |
| **RL-based MDO** | Policy networks replace EI solver |
| **Physics-informed NNs** | Embed governing equations in surrogate |
| **Automatic differentiation** | Exact gradients through any Julia BB |

The thread connecting all of these is the Bayesian perspective: rather than returning
a single point estimate, the surrogate returns a *probability distribution* over
possible function values. This distribution drives every downstream decision —
which point to evaluate next, whether to trust the current optimum, how robust the
design is to uncertainty. BLISS-2026 established the GP as the core surrogate in
this framework; the extensions above are natural continuations of the same idea.
